segments.text: So I drove a Mach-E for two and a half years and absolutely loved it, but I lived in Texas
and we were driving back and forth to Nebraska a lot and my husband's like,
we can't take the time to charge because we stopped twice.
But I'm telling you, I was driving an ICE vehicle now, I forget to put gas in
because I used to just plug it in every night and it was just, it was so easy.
And I'll tell people, I have the perfect scenario for like, I live in Strongville,
which is 20 miles north of here.
I have a son in Omaha, a son in Lincoln, a daughter in Omaha, and a son in Aurora.
So we're going 40-50 miles any one way, any day, but I get back home.
And the other thing is, except with my commute to Columbus, it's just the perfect scenario.
I will say though, road tripping in Mach-E is not as good because they're in Coral.
Whereas Tesla, I've heard, you know, they're 250 plus KW charging.
Kia, Hyundai, I think are pretty high charging.
So as technology gets better, we'll charge faster and be better road trippers.
But that was the most fun I've ever had driving was in that car.
I'm very much a rural follower and I do traffic safety for a living.
And so my husband's like, I've never seen you exceed the speed limit until you've got that car.
But they are so fun to drive.
I think that's got people off of that.

So I'm just showing you a couple of pictures of some manual disconnects just to talk about the fact that they exist.
And this is a bail style.
So you're going to actually grab this lever and you're going to pull the lever down.
As you pull the lever down, it actually walks the connector out for you.
If you were to put it back, as you push that bail closed, it's going to walk the actual connector in.
That saves damage on the terminals.
You don't have people misaligning and then shoving them in.
So this would be an example.
This is like an early, this was an escape.
They just had literally a switch.
And so all I'm really trying to do is show you, hey, you know what?
Over the years, there's been lots of different examples of where they are and what they are.
And so I never think, I'm never a fan of encouraging first responders to use manual disconnects.
The manual disconnect is more for tow companies potentially or definitely long-term storage.
If you're going to take a vehicle that's been in an accident, that's got high-voltage batteries, especially an EV that's got lithium batteries,
let's talk for a bit about storage.
What are you going to do with that vehicle?
Because there is a risk of fires starting or igniting long after the vehicle crash occurred.
I'll start with the primary reason why one of these would catch fires is because there's physical damage to the battery.
So if there's physical damage to the battery and the electrolyte can start to come out of the cells, that's where we have big problems.
A lot of times after a crash, it's not super feasible to decide if there's any damage to it or not.
You can kind of look it over and say probably not, but you don't have to be sure.
So let's say you're storing a vehicle again after a crash.
You ought to be minimum of 50 foot from any other structures, objects that you don't want to run.
Luckily, in our part of the world, in Nebraska, we can probably find a place to put this.
This is going to be a bigger problem as you get deeper and deeper into urban areas.
Some of the cities are actually building what really look like small silage storage.
If you kind of picture the cement walls that you could pack silage into,
they're basically building little structures like that where they could put this vehicle and let it sit
or at least they've got cement barriers around it.
I was reading about, I couldn't find a picture of one,
but I was reading how I think the L.A. Police Department is doing that
because they need to be prepared to hold a vehicle as evidence.
So now they've got a vehicle that came out of a collision.
They don't want to put it where the general public can come up and tamper, get into it, whatever.
So they've got to put it somewhere where they've got control of the vehicle
and they've got to put it where it's not going to catch something on fire and create a problem that way.
So those are all probably long-term considerations to think about what's the best approach.
The worst thing you can do is take an EV vehicle that's been in a collision
and bring it inside your building and store it.
Store them outside and you've got to make space for them.
I do, I guess, just show you what the PPE, if you're working with a vehicle that you feel like you cannot get energized,
this is the PPE that you wear.
They're a rubber glove that has to be protective up to 10,000 volts.
And then you've got to put a leather glove over the top so that you can't just poke through.
Pain in the butt to work with.
But when you need them, you want to utilize them.
Now the good news is, I said it before and I'll say it one more time,
first responders really should, I don't have a good scenario where a first responder really should not have to deal with this.
